Item 1. Business.
Overview
JPMorgan Chase & Co. ("JPMorgan Chase" or the "Firm", NYSE: JPM), a financial holding company incorporated under Delaware law in 1968, is a leading financial services firm based in the United States of America ("U.S."), with operations worldwide. JPMorgan Chase had $3.7 trillion in assets and $292.3 billion in stockholders' equity as of December 31, 2022. The Firm is a leader in investment banking, financial services for consumers and small businesses, commercial banking, financial transaction processing and asset management. Under the J.P. Morgan and Chase brands, the Firm serves millions of customers, predominantly in the U.S., and many of the world's most prominent corporate, institutional and government clients globally.
JPMorgan Chase's principal bank subsidiary is JPMorgan Chase Bank, National Association ("JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A."), a national banking association with U.S. branches in 48 states and Washington, D.C. JPMorgan Chase's principal non-bank subsidiary is J.P. Morgan Securities LLC ("J.P. Morgan Securities"), a U.S. broker-dealer. The bank and non-bank subsidiaries of JPMorgan Chase operate nationally as well as through overseas branches and subsidiaries, representative offices and subsidiary foreign banks. The Firm's principal operating subsidiaries outside the U.S. are J.P. Morgan Securities plc and J.P. Morgan SE ("JPMSE"), which are subsidiaries of JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. and are based in the United Kingdom ("U.K.") and Germany, respectively.
The Firm's website is www.jpmorganchase.com. JPMorgan Chase makes available on its website, free of charge, annual reports on Form 10-K, quarterly reports on Form 10-Q and current reports on Form 8-K pursuant to Section 13(a) or Section 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as soon as reasonably practicable after it electronically files or furnishes such material to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC") at www.sec.gov. JPMorgan Chase makes new and important information about the Firm available on its website at https://www.jpmorganchase.com, including on the Investor Relations section of its website at https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir. Information on the Firm's website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2022 ("2022 Form 10-K" or "Form 10-K") or the Firm's other filings with the SEC. The Firm has adopted, and posted on its website, a Code of Conduct for all employees of the Firm and a Code of Ethics for its Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, Chief Financial Officer, Principal Accounting Officer and all other professionals of the Firm worldwide serving in a finance, accounting, treasury, tax or investor relations role. The Code of Ethics is also available in print upon request to the Firm's Investor Relations team. Within the time period required by the SEC, JPMorgan Chase will post on its website any amendment to the Code of Ethics and any waiver applicable to a director or executive officer.
Business segments
For management reporting purposes, JPMorgan Chase's activities are organized into four major reportable business segments, as well as a Corporate segment. The Firm's consumer business is the Consumer & Community Banking ("CCB") segment. The Firm's wholesale business segments are the Corporate & Investment Bank ("CIB"), Commercial Banking ("CB"), and Asset & Wealth Management ("AWM").
A description of the Firm's business segments and the products and services they provide to their respective client bases is provided in the "Business segment results" section of Management's discussion and analysis of financial condition and results of operations ("Management's discussion and analysis" or "MD&A"), beginning on page 46 and in Note 32.
Competition
JPMorgan Chase and its subsidiaries and affiliates operate in highly competitive environments. Competitors include other banks, brokerage firms, investment banking companies, merchant banks, hedge funds, commodity trading companies, private equity firms, insurance companies, mutual fund companies, investment managers, credit card companies, mortgage banking companies, trust companies, securities processing companies, automobile financing companies, leasing companies, e-commerce and other internet-based companies, financial technology companies, and other companies engaged in providing similar as well as new products and services. The Firm's businesses generally compete on the basis of the quality and variety of the Firm's products and services, transaction execution, innovation, reputation and price. Competition also varies based on the types of clients, customers, industries and geographies served. With respect to some of its geographies and products, JPMorgan Chase competes globally; with respect to others, the Firm competes on a national or regional basis. New competitors in the financial services industry continue to emerge, including firms that offer products and services solely through the internet and non-financial companies that offer products and services that disintermediate traditional banking products and services offered by financial services firms such as JPMorgan Chase.

JPM.quantitative_report:
# JPM Quantitative Report — Decision Date 2023-07-01

## 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** has accelerated for five consecutive quarters: $30.72B (Q1'22) → $30.71B → $32.72B → $34.55B → $38.35B (Q1'23). QoQ growth was +11.0%; YoY growth was +24.8%. TTM revenue stands at $136.33B.

**EPS** followed a similar inflection after a trough in Q1'22 ($2.63): $2.76 → $3.12 → $3.58 → $4.10 (Q1'23). QoQ EPS growth was +14.5%; YoY was +55.9%. TTM net income is $42.02B.

**Margins** are expanding. Q1'23 operating income of $15.97B on $38.35B revenue implies a quarterly operating margin of ~41.6%, above the trailing 38.2% reported. Net margin is 30.8%. ROE has improved to 13.9% from 12.9% in the prior three quarters.

**Balance sheet**: Total equity grew from $285.90B (Q1'22) to $303.08B (Q1'23), a +6.0% increase over eight quarters. Total assets are $3,744.30B, implying a leverage ratio (assets/equity) of 12.4x—broadly stable versus the prior range of 12.8–13.8x. Equity has increased for three consecutive quarters.

**Cash flow**: No cash-flow data are supplied; cash generation cannot be assessed.

## 2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience

Earnings quality indicators are mixed due to data gaps. Net margin has been stable in the 0.30–0.35 range across all eight quarters, and the latest 30.8% sits at the midpoint. ROE at 13.9% is the highest in the eight-quarter sample, up from 12.9% in the prior three quarters, suggesting improving capital efficiency rather than balance-sheet bloat. Operating margin at 38.2% (trailing) and ~41.6% (Q1'23) indicates strong operating leverage on the revenue ramp. Without cash-flow statements, accruals quality and free-cash-flow conversion cannot be verified—this is a material gap.

Balance-sheet resilience: equity has grown while total assets have declined from a peak of $3,954.69B (Q1'22) to $3,744.30B, reducing leverage. The equity cushion of $303.08B against $3,441.22B in liabilities is consistent with a well-capitalized large bank, though no regulatory capital ratios are supplied.

## 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

| Metric | Latest (2023-06-30) | 12-Month Range | Percentile |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E (diluted) | 10.29 | 7.6 – 11.9 | ~62nd |
| P/B | 1.427 | 1.1 – 1.5 | ~82nd |
| P/S | 3.171 | n/a history | — |
| Earnings yield | 9.7% | — | — |
| Net margin | 30.8% | 0.30 – 0.40 | ~9th |
| ROE | 13.9% | 0.10 – 0.14 | ~98th |

P/E at 10.29x is mid-range versus the 7.6–11.9x window, not at a trough. P/B at 1.427x is near the top of its 1.1–1.5x range, which is notable given that ROE is simultaneously at its highest observed level (13.9%). The P/B expansion appears to be pricing in the ROE improvement. Earnings yield of 9.7% is attractive in absolute terms but must be weighed against the absence of cash-flow confirmation and the cyclicality inherent in bank earnings.

## 4. Price Level and Return Context Versus Fundamentals

**Price**: $145.44 (2023-06-30), within 0.4% of the 52-week high of $146.00 and 43.6% above the 52-week low of $101.28.

**Returns**: Quarterly (≈63 trading days) +13.0%; annual (≈252 trading days) +26.1%.

**Quarterly path** shows significant volatility: -6.3% (Q2'22) → +22.7% (Q3'22) → -2.8% (Q4'22) → +13.0% (Q1'23). The stock has recovered strongly from the Q4'22 drawdown.

**Fundamental alignment**: The +26.1% annual price return is well below the +55.9% YoY EPS growth, suggesting the market has not fully re-rated the earnings acceleration—P/E compressed from ~10.4x a year ago to 10.3x currently. However, P/B expanded from ~1.19x to 1.43x over the same period, so the equity multiple has already moved meaningfully higher.

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside

**Upside**:
- Revenue momentum is strong: five consecutive quarters of growth, with Q1'23 +24.8% YoY. If sustained, TTM earnings could push higher, and P/E could re-rate toward the upper end of its 11.9x range. At 11.9x on Q1'23 annualized EPS of $16.40 ($4.10 × 4), the implied price is ~$195, a +34% move from $145.44.
- ROE at 13.9% is at the top of the observed range; if it sustains or improves, P/B could hold or expand from 1.43x.

**Downside**:
- P/E has a floor of 7.6x in the supplied window. At 7.6x on the same annualized EPS, the implied price is ~$125, a −14% move. If earnings mean-revert (Q1'22 EPS was $2.63, implying annualized ~$10.5), the downside is materially worse.
- P/B at 1.427x is near the top of its range; reversion to the 1.1x floor would imply ~$113 on current book value per share, a −22% move.
- The absence of cash-flow data means the quality of the earnings ramp is unverified; a credit-cycle turn could compress both margins and multiples simultaneously.

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

JPM exhibits clear positive revenue and earnings momentum with expanding margins and a strengthening balance sheet. However, the stock is trading near its 52-week high, P/B is at the 82nd percentile of its range, and P/E is mid-range at 10.3x. The +55.9% YoY EPS growth has not fully re-rated the P/E, but the P/B has already moved from 1.19x to 1.43x, capturing much of the ROE improvement.

The asymmetry is moderate: upside to the P/E ceiling (11.9x) offers ~34% on annualized earnings, while downside to the P/E floor (7.6x) risks ~14%—but the floor scenario likely coincides with earnings deterioration, amplifying the drawdown. The missing cash-flow data is a material blind spot for assessing earnings durability. On the supplied numbers alone, the risk/reward is balanced to slightly favorable, contingent on the sustainability of the Q1'23 earnings run-rate, which cannot be confirmed from the available evidence.
JPM.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Assessment Report: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM)
**Decision Date: 2023-07-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Description**
Based on the 2022 Form 10-K (filing date: 2023-02-21), JPMorgan Chase is a leading global financial services firm with $3.7 trillion in assets. Its operations are organized into four segments: Consumer & Community Banking (CCB), Corporate & Investment Bank (CIB), Commercial Banking (CB), and Asset & Wealth Management (AWM). The firm is a leader in investment banking, consumer and commercial banking, transaction processing, and asset management. It operates in highly competitive environments facing competition from traditional banks, fintech firms, and other financial service providers, competing on product quality, innovation, reputation, and price.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated & Supplied)**
*   **2023-06-30:** All 23 major U.S. banks, including JPMorgan Chase, passed the Federal Reserve's annual stress test, indicating resilience in a hypothetical severe recession. This result was widely cited as a positive catalyst for the bank sector (multiple sources, 2023-06-29/30).
*   **2023-06-30:** Market commentary predicted a possible dividend increase from JPM following the stress test clearance, expected as early as Friday, June 30 (CNBC Final Trade, 2023-06-30).
*   **2023-06-05:** JPMorgan Chase agreed to pay $307 million in penalties to settle SEC and federal bank regulator charges over undisclosed conflicts of interest in its wealth management business (Washington Post, 2023-06-05).
*   **2023-06-22:** The bank agreed to pay a $4 million fine to the SEC to settle allegations it mistakenly and permanently deleted 47 million electronic records, violating federal rules (SEC release/Business Standard, 2023-06-22).
*   **2023-05-31:** JPMorgan is pursuing an aggressive, tech-focused growth strategy, including expanding its U.S. branch footprint for customer acquisition and launching an AI-powered investment advice solution (eMarketer, 2023-05-31). It announced a $15 billion annual technology budget in May 2023 (Business Insider, 2023-05-25).
*   **2023-05-26:** JPMorgan faces ongoing lawsuits related to its historical ties to Jeffrey Epstein (Fox Business, 2023-05-26).

**3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    1.  **Regulatory & Litigation:** Recent settlements ($307M for conflicts, $4M for recordkeeping) demonstrate tangible operational and compliance risk. The Epstein-related lawsuits present additional ongoing reputational and financial litigation risk.
    2.  **Competitive Pressure:** The 10-K identifies intense competition from fintech and non-traditional players disintermediating traditional banking services.
*   **Offsets:**
    1.  **Capital Strength & System Importance:** Passing the 2023 Fed stress test confirms the firm's resilience and strong capital position, facilitating shareholder returns.
    2.  **Scale & Strategic Investment:** The firm's scale ($3.7T assets) and stated commitment to significant technology investment ($15B annually) provide a platform to defend against competition and pursue growth, as highlighted in recent strategy updates.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral.** The firm's franchise remains fundamentally strong, evidenced by its global leadership across multiple business lines and its clean result from the 2023 regulatory stress test. However, this positive is balanced by a recent pattern of material regulatory fines and ongoing legal liabilities, which point to operational and reputational risks. The strategic push in technology and branch expansion is a forward-looking positive, but its impact is not yet quantifiably evidenced in the supplied text. The near-term catalyst (stress test, potential dividend) is positive but counterbalanced by the recent settlement news. The qualitative outlook is, therefore, balanced.

*Evidence Confirmed: All points are directly sourced from the supplied 10-K text, dated news summaries, and web context snippets. Inferences are limited to connecting the stated stress test pass to resilience and dividend capacity.*

Item 1. Business
General
Cisco designs and sells a broad range of technologies that power the Internet. We are integrating our platforms across networking, security, collaboration, applications and the cloud. These platforms are designed to help our customers manage more users, devices and things connecting to their networks. This will enable us to provide customers with a highly secure, intelligent platform for their digital business.

In addition to our product offerings, we provide a broad range of service offerings, including technical support services and advanced services. Increasingly, we are delivering our technologies through software and services. Our customers include businesses of all sizes, public institutions, governments, and se

hardware support to include software, solutions, and premium support.
We also provide comprehensive advisory services that are focused on responsive, preventive, and consultative support of our technologies for specific networking needs. We are investing in and expanding advisory services in the areas of software, cloud, security, and analytics, which reflects our strategy of selling customer outcomes. We are focused on three priorities: utilizing technology advisory services to drive higher product and services; assessment and migration services providing the tools, expertise and methodologies to enable our customers to migrate to new technology platforms; and providing optimization services aligned with customers' business expectations.
5

Customers and Markets
Many factors influence the IT, collaboration, and networking requirements of our customers. These include the size of the organization, number and types of technology systems, geographic location, and business applications deployed throughout the customer's network. Our customer base is not limited to any specific industry, geography, or market segment. Our customers primarily operate in the following markets: enterprise, commercial, service provider, and public sector.
Enterprise
Enterprise businesses are large regional, national, or global organizations with multiple locations or branch offices. Many enterprise businesses have unique IT, collaboration, and networking needs within a multivendor environment. We offer service and support packages, financing, and managed network services, primarily through our service provider partners. We sell these products through a network of third-party application and technology vendors and channel partners, as well as selling directly to these customers.
Commercial
The commercial market represents larger, or midmarket and small businesses. We sell to our midmarket customers through a combination of our direct sales force and channel partners. These customers typically require the latest advanced technologies that our enterprise customers demand, but with less complexity. Small businesses require information technologies and communications products that are easy to configure, install, and maintain. We sell to these smaller organizations within the commercial market primarily through channel partners.
Service Providers
Service providers offer data, voice, video, and mobile/wireless services to businesses, governments, utilities, and consumers worldwide. The service provider market includes regional, national, and international wireline carriers, webscale operators as well as Internet, cable, and wireless providers. We also include media, broadcast, and content providers within our service provider market, as the lines in the telecommunications industry continue to blur between traditional network-based, content-based and application-based services. Service providers use a variety of our products and services for their own networks. In addition, many service providers use Cisco data center, virtualization, and collaboration technologies to offer managed or Internet-based services to their business customers. Compared with other customers, service providers are more likely to require network design, deployment, and support services because of the greater scale and higher complexity of their networks, whose requirements are addressed, we believe, by our architectural approach.
Public Sector
The public sector market includes federal, state and local governments, as well as educational institution customers. Many public sector customers have unique IT, collaboration, and networking needs within a multi-vendor environment. We sell to public sector customers through a network of third-party application and technology vendors, channel partners, as well as through direct sales.
Sales Overview
As of the end of fiscal 2022, our worldwide sales and marketing functions consisted of approximately 26,000 employees, including managers, sales representatives, and technical support personnel. We sell our products and services both directly and through a variety of channels with support from our salesforce. A substantial portion of our products and services is sold through channel partners, and the remainder is sold through direct sales. Channel partners include systems integrators, service providers, other resellers, and distributors.
Systems integrators and service providers typically sell directly to end users and often provide system installation, technical support, professional services, and other support services in addition to network equipment sales. Systems integrators also typically integrate our products into an overall solution. Some service providers are also systems integrators.
Distributors may hold inventory and sell to systems integrators, service providers, and other resellers. We refer to sales through distributors as our two-tier system of sales to the end customer. Revenue from two-tier distributors is recognized based on a sell-in method. These distributors may be given business terms that allow them to return a limited portion of inventory, receive credits for changes in selling prices, receive certain rebates, and participate in various cooperative marketing programs.

CSCO.quantitative_report:
# CSCO Quantitative Report — Decision Date 2023-07-01

## 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** has accelerated meaningfully. Quarterly revenue rose from $12.84B (Q3 2022) to $14.57B (Q3 2023), a YoY gain of +13.5%. The QoQ step from Q2 to Q3 2023 was +7.2% ($13.59B → $14.57B). TTM revenue stands at $54.90B.

**Earnings** recovered after a trough. EPS diluted fell from $0.73 (Q3 2022) to $0.65 (Q1 2023) before rebounding to $0.78 (Q3 2023), a YoY increase of +6.8% and QoQ jump of +16.4%. TTM net income is $11.47B.

**Margins** dipped and are recovering:
- Gross margin: 63.3% in Q3 2023 ($9.23B / $14.57B), up from a trough of 61.3% in Q3–Q4 2022; TTM gross margin = 62.0%.
- Operating margin: 27.1% in Q3 2023 ($3.95B / $14.57B), rebounding from 24.2% in Q1 2023; TTM operating margin = 25.9%.
- Net margin: 22.0% in Q3 2023 ($3.21B / $14.57B), up from 19.6% trough in Q4 2022; TTM net margin = 20.9%.

**Cash flow:** No quarterly cash-flow data supplied. Free cash flow and operating cash flow are unavailable for all eight quarters.

**Balance sheet** is strengthening. Total debt declined from $11.50B (Jul 2021) to $8.40B (Apr 2023). Total equity grew from $41.27B to $42.30B over the same span. Total assets are stable at $97.53B. Debt-to-equity = 0.199.

## 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality** is mixed. Net margin has compressed from ~23% (fiscal 2021–2022) to ~20–22%, suggesting cost pressure or mix shift despite top-line growth. ROE has declined from 0.297 (Jul 2022) to 0.271 (Jun 2023). The absence of cash-flow data prevents assessment of accruals or cash conversion; this is a material gap.

**Balance-sheet resilience** is high. Debt-to-equity of 0.199 is conservative. Total debt has been reduced by $3.10B over eight quarters (−27%). Equity is growing. No leverage red flags.

## 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

| Metric | Current (Jun 2023) | 1-Year Range | Position in Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | 18.54 | 13.9 – 19.1 | 89th percentile |
| P/B | 5.028 | 4.1 – 5.2 | 85th percentile |
| P/S | 3.874 | n/a | — |
| Earnings yield | 5.4% | n/a | — |
| Net margin | 20.9% | 20% – 20% | Low end of range |
| ROE | 27.1% | 27% – 30% | Low end of range |

The P/E has expanded from 14.3 (Oct 2022) to 18.54, a +30% multiple expansion. The stock is trading near the top of its one-year valuation band on both P/E and P/B, while profitability metrics (net margin, ROE) sit at the low end of their ranges. Valuation is stretched relative to the company's own recent history, and the multiple expansion has outpaced the earnings recovery.

## 4. Price Level & Return Context Versus Fundamentals

- **Current price:** $51.74 (Jun 30, 2023)
- **52-week range:** $38.60 – $52.56; price is at the 94th percentile of its range.
- **Annual return:** +20.7% (from $42.88, Jun 2022)
- **Quarterly return:** +0.6% (from $51.43, Mar 2023) — essentially flat.
- **Quarterly path:** +13.9% (Q3 2022), +9.3% (Q4 2022), +0.6% (Q1 2023) — momentum has decelerated sharply.

The +20.7% annual price gain is explained by a combination of modest EPS growth (+6.8% YoY) and significant P/E expansion (14.8 → 18.5). The recent quarterly flattening of price coincides with the P/E reaching the top of its range, even as revenue growth accelerated. Price has outrun the fundamental improvement.

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside:**
- Revenue acceleration is real: +13.5% YoY is the strongest in the eight-quarter series, and operating margin rebounded to 27.1% from a 24.2% trough, suggesting operating leverage is re-engaging.
- Balance sheet strength (D/E = 0.199, declining debt) provides capital allocation flexibility.
- If the revenue growth trajectory sustains and margins stabilize near current levels, TTM EPS could grow toward $3.20+ (vs. current run-rate ~$3.12), supporting the current multiple.

**Downside:**
- P/E of 18.54 is at the 89th percentile of its one-year range; mean reversion to the median (~16.5) implies ~11% downside even with flat earnings.
- Net margin (20.9%) and ROE (27.1%) are at the low end of their ranges despite revenue growth, raising questions about structural margin compression.
- No cash-flow data means quality of earnings cannot be verified; any cash-flow deterioration would be a negative surprise.
- Price momentum has stalled (+0.6% quarterly) while the stock sits at 94% of its 52-week range, limiting near-term technical support for further re-rating.

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

CSCO presents a **balanced but valuation-constrained** profile. The fundamental trajectory is improving — revenue growth is accelerating (+13.5% YoY), margins are recovering from a trough, and the balance sheet is strong (D/E 0.199). However, the stock has already priced much of this improvement: the P/E has expanded 30% from its trough to 18.54, near the top of its one-year range, while profitability metrics (ROE 27.1%, net margin 20.9%) sit at the bottom of theirs. The annual price return of +20.7% has significantly exceeded the +6.8% EPS growth, indicating multiple expansion rather than earnings-driven gains. With quarterly price momentum essentially flat and the stock at 94% of its 52-week high, the risk/reward skews toward limited upside from current levels unless revenue acceleration drives a further earnings re-rating — a scenario that requires margin stability the numbers have not yet consistently demonstrated. The absence of cash-flow data is a material evidentiary gap.
CSCO.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Cisco Systems (CSCO)**
**Date of Evidence:** Pre-2023-07-01

### 1. Business/Franchise Read
Cisco is a global technology leader that designs and sells a broad range of technologies "that power the Internet." Its core strategy is to help customers "connect, secure, and automate to accelerate their digital agility in a cloud-first world" (2022-09-08 10-K). The business is structured around key customer priorities: Reimagine Applications, Power Hybrid Work, Transform Infrastructure, and Secure the Enterprise (2022-09-08 10-K).

The franchise is built on an integrated portfolio spanning networking, security, collaboration, and cloud management. Key pillars include its "Secure, Agile Networks" (switching, routing, wireless) segment, which forms its traditional backbone, and a strategic push into "End-to-End Security" and "Optimized Application Experiences" through observability tools (2022-09-08 10-K). The company is actively transforming its business model from hardware-centric to software and subscription-based (e.g., Cisco DNA, Webex, SASE) to drive recurring revenue (2022-09-08 10-K). Its sales are global, serving enterprises, commercial businesses, service providers, and the public sector through a mix of direct sales and channel partners (2022-09-08 10-K).

### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)
*   **Financial Performance & Guidance (2023-05-18):** Cisco reported Q3 fiscal 2023 results that beat earnings and revenue estimates. The company boosted its full-year guidance, with the CEO noting strong performance in the public sector business (2023-05-18). It expects Q4 revenue growth of 14-16% year-over-year and full-year growth of 10-10.5%, representing acceleration from prior years (2023-05-21).
*   **Strategic Pivot to AI & Security (2023-05-17):** The CEO articulated growth from security, software, and generative AI, calling security "a top priority for the company" and promising new innovations in coming quarters (2023-05-17).
*   **Product Innovation in AI Networking (2023-06-22):** Cisco unveiled a "speedy AI networking chip," explicitly targeting the data movement needs for AI model training (2023-06-22). This positions the company in the AI infrastructure build-out, with another article noting it wants "in on the AI race" challenging Broadcom and Marvell (2023-06-28).
*   **Supply Chain (2023-05-15):** It was noted that easing supply chain constraints were expected to benefit Cisco's Q3 results (2023-05-15).

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets
*   **Risk: Intensifying Competition.** The move to design AI chips (2023-06-22, 2023-06-28) places Cisco in competition with established semiconductor players like Broadcom and Marvell, potentially straining R&D resources. The broader networking and security markets remain highly competitive.
*   **Risk: Execution & Strategic Pivot.** The stated strategy requires successfully transitioning to software/subscriptions and capturing share in new growth areas (security, AI). This involves significant R&D investment and potential dilution of focus from core networking.
*   **Risk: Geopolitical & Operational.** The 10-K details risks from global operations, including geopolitical tensions (noted context on Ukraine, 2023-06-20), currency fluctuations, and complex supply chains (2022-09-08 10-K). Recent news includes a lawsuit over the "iPhone" trademark (2023-05-19) and a Tax Court ruling on a fraud penalty related to stock options (2023-06-22).
*   **Offset: Strong Demand & Guidance.** The raised financial guidance (2023-05-18) and easing supply chains (2023-05-15) indicate sustained demand and operational improvement. Analyst ratings skew neutral-to-bullish with an average price target implying upside (2023-06-30).
*   **Offset: Positioned in Secular Growth Trends.** Leadership in enterprise networking, a broad security portfolio (2023-05-17), and new AI-centric product launches (2023-06-22) align Cisco with critical enterprise spending priorities on security, cloud, and AI infrastructure.

### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Neutral, with firm-specific positive momentum offset by sector-wide competitive risks.**

The evidence presents a company at a strategic inflection point. **Positively,** recent financial results (2023-05-18) and improved guidance suggest strong execution on its existing portfolio and improved supply conditions. The proactive, public pivot toward high-growth areas like Security and AI networking (2023-05-17, 2023-06-22) is a constructive strategic response to market trends, aiming to leverage its vast installed base and channel relationships. **Negatively,** this pivot increases competitive exposure to well-entrenched rivals in semiconductors and pure-play security/software firms. The risks inherent in business model transition and global operations (2022-09-08 10-K) remain material. The near-term catalyst from earnings and guidance is balanced by the long-term question of execution against formidable competition. Therefore, the qualitative outlook is **neutral,** pending evidence of tangible success in capturing market share in its new prioritized domains.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Cisco's fundamentals are genuinely improving—Q3 2023 revenue grew +13.5% YoY to $14.57B, margins are rebounding, and the balance sheet is strong with D/E of 0.199. However, the stock has already priced in much of this recovery: P/E has expanded 30% to 18.54 (89th percentile of its one-year range) while the price sits at 94% of its 52-week high, leaving limited upside unless the revenue acceleration sustains and margins stabilize at higher levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Cisco's fundamentals are genuinely improving—Q3 2023 revenue grew +13.5% YoY to $14.57B, margins are rebounding, and the balance sheet is strong with D/E of 0.199. However, the stock has already priced in much of this recovery: P/E has expanded 30% to 18.54 (89th percentile of its one-year range) while the price sits at 94% of its 52-week high, leaving limited upside unless the revenue acceleration sustains and margins stabilize at higher levels.
key_factors: Revenue acceleration is real at +13.5% YoY (Q3 2023), the strongest in the eight-quarter series, with operating margin rebounding to 27.1% from a 24.2% trough—suggesting operating leverage is re-engaging.

P/E has expanded from 14.3 (Oct 2022) to 18.54, a +30% multiple expansion that has outpaced the +6.8% YoY EPS growth, placing the stock at the 89th percentile of its one-year valuation range while profitability metrics (ROE 27.1%, net margin 20.9%) sit at the low end of theirs.

Strategic pivot toward AI networking (new AI networking chip unveiled 2023-06-22) and security (CEO called it 'a top priority' on 2023-05-17) aligns Cisco with secular enterprise spending trends, but execution risk against entrenched competitors like Broadcom and Marvell is material.

Balance sheet strength (D/E = 0.199, debt reduced by $3.1B over eight quarters) provides capital allocation flexibility, but the absence of cash-flow data prevents verification of earnings quality—a material evidentiary gap.

ITEM 1.    Business
OVERVIEW
Visa is one of the world's leaders in digital payments. Our purpose is to uplift everyone, everywhere by being the best way to pay and be paid. We facilitate global commerce and money movement across more than 200 countries and territories among a global set of consumers, merchants, financial institutions and government entities through innovative technologies.
Since Visa's early days in 1958, we have been in the business of facilitating payments between consumers and businesses. As a trusted engine of commerce and with new ways to pay, we are working to provide payment solutions for everyone, everywhere. We are focused on extending, enhancing and investing in our proprietary network, VisaNet, to offer a single connection point for facilitating payment transactions to multiple endpoints through various form factors. Through our network, we offer products, solutions and services that facilitate secure, reliable and efficient money movement for participants in the ecosystem.
- We facilitate secure, reliable and efficient money movement among consumers, issuing and acquiring financial institutions, and merchants. We have traditionally referred to this as the "four-party" model. Please see Our Core Business discussion below. As the payments ecosystem continues to evolve, we have broadened this model to include digital banks, digital wallets and a range of financial technology companies (fintechs), governments and non-governmental organizations (NGOs). We provide transaction processing services (primarily authorization, clearing and settlement) to our financial institution and merchant clients through VisaNet, our advanced transaction processing network. During fiscal year 2022, we saw 258 billion payments and cash transactions with Visa's brand, equating to an average of 707 million transactions per day. Of the 258 billion total transactions, 193 billion were processed by Visa.
- We offer a wide range of Visa-branded payment products that our clients, including nearly 15,000 financial institutions, use to develop and offer core business solutions, including credit, debit, prepaid and cash access programs for individual, business and government account holders. During fiscal year 2022, Visa's total payments and cash volume was $14 trillion, and 4.1 billion credentials(1) were available worldwide to be used at more than 80 million merchant locations, plus an estimated 20 million locations through payment facilitators.(1)
- We take an open partnership approach and seek to provide value by enabling access to our global network, including offering our technology capabilities through application programming interfaces (APIs). We partner with both traditional and emerging players to innovate and expand the payments ecosystem, allowing them to leverage the resources of our platform to scale and grow their businesses more quickly and effectively.
- We are accelerating the migration to digital payments and continue to evolve to be a "network of networks" to enable the movement of money through all available networks. We aim to provide a single connection point so that Visa clients can enable money movement for businesses, governments and consu

In line with our commitment to an expansive and diverse range of partnerships for the benefit of our stakeholders, Visa is the only brand in the world that is a top sponsor of FIFA, the

Talent
Attracting, developing and advancing the best talent globally is critical to our continued success. This year we grew our total workforce from approximately 21,500 in fiscal year 2021 to approximately 26,500 employees in fiscal year 2022, an increase of 23 percent year over year. Voluntary workforce turnover (rolling 12-month attrition) was 12.1 percent as of September 30, 2022. Visa employees are located in more than 80 countries and territories, with 54 percent located outside the U.S. At the end of fiscal year 2022, Visa's global workforce was 58 percent men and 42 percent women, and women represented 36 percent of Visa's leadership (defined as vice president level and above). In the U.S., ethnicity of our workforce was 41 percent Asian, 8 percent Black, 12 percent Hispanic, 3 percent Other and 36 percent White. For our U.S. leadership, the breakdown was 18 percent Asian, 7 percent Black, 13 percent Hispanic, 2 percent Other and 60 percent White.
Given Visa's ambitious growth agenda, it is important to enable our employees to achieve their individual performance goals while also supporting personal career interests. This year we introduced several changes to career growth and planning at Visa, including new growth paths and tools that take into consideration the unique professional backgrounds, skills, accomplishments, and future performance goals of our employees. These tools support meaningful dialogue about performance and help drive development, retention and growth of top talent in a highly competitive talent market.
We have an unwavering commitment to valuing the unique identities of our employees and their contributions to Visa. In 2020, we established the Stand Together initiative in support of social justice and racial equality in the U.S. focused on our people, our community and our company. We are proud of the progress we have made. Our partnership with the Thurgood Marshall College Fund for the Visa Black Scholars and Jobs Program resulted in Visa's inaugural class of 51 scholars participating in year-round programs and training aimed at developing their professional and technical skills this past year. We also welcomed the second cohort of 75 scholars this fall. Upon graduation, all scholars who have met their commitments will be offered a full-time job with Visa.
We continue to increase the number of underrepresented employees in the U.S. We are committed to recruiting and retaining diverse talent through employee development programs aimed at advancing their careers at Visa. As a company, we continue to partner with historically Black colleges and a generally more diverse set of universities to further develop our talent pipeline. Visa is committed to pay equity, regardless of gender or race/ethnicity, and conducts pay equity analyses on an annual basis. More details regarding our human capital management, as well as enhanced workforce disclosures that include our 2021 Consolidated EEO-1 Report and our 2021 Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Report, can be found on our website at visa.com/esg. See Available Information below.
For additional information, please see the section titled "Talent and Human Capital Management" in Visa's 2022 Proxy Statement.
FINTECH AND DIGITAL PARTNERSHIPS
Fintechs are key enablers of new payment experiences and new flows. Our work with fintechs has opened new points of acceptance, extended credit at the point of sale, made cross-border money flows more efficient, moved B2B spend onto Visa's network, expedited payroll and provided digital wallet customers access to our services.
To better serve fintechs, Visa has a suite of streamlined commercial programs and digital onboarding tools. Our Fintech Fast Track program enables qualifying fintechs to quickly launch and scale their programs. The program has welcomed hundreds of fintechs who are actively engaged in the program.
With our startup engagement programs, the Visa Everywhere Initiative and the Inclusive Fintech 50, early-stage companies can build payment solutions based on our capabilities. With the global Visa Fintech Partner Connect program, we are helping our clients tap into innovations emerging from the fintech community. The program is designed to help financial institutions quickly connect with a curated set of technology providers, helping Visa's issuing partners create digital-first experiences.

We rely on a combination of patent, trademark, copyright and trade secret laws in the U.S. and other jurisdictions, as well as confidentiality procedures and contractual provisions, to protect our proprietary technology.

Existing and emerging competitors compete with Visa's network and payment solutions for consumers and for participation by financial institutions and merchants. Technology and innovation are shifting consumer habits and driving growth opportunities in ecommerce, mobile payments, blockchain technology and digital currencies. **Quantitative Report: Visa (V) as of 2023-07-01**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
- **Revenue:** Steady quarterly growth from $6.13B (Q3 2021) to $7.99B (Q2 2023). TTM revenue = $30.98B. YoY growth = +11.1%, QoQ growth = +0.6%.
- **Earnings:** Net income grew from $2.58B (Q3 2021) to $4.26B (Q2 2023). TTM net income = $15.79B, TTM operating income = $19.66B. YoY net income growth = +16.7%.
- **Margins:** Net margin stable at 0.51 (51%) over the 365-day window. Operating margin = 0.635 (63.5%).
- **Cash-Flow:** Unavailable (no data supplied).
- **Balance Sheet:** Total assets grew to $86.75B (Q2 2023) from $82.67B (Q3 2021). Total equity increased to $38.56B (Q2 2023) from $37.79B (Q3 2021). Total liabilities decreased to $48.19B (Q2 2023) from $49.93B (Q3 2022). Debt data only available for 2021: total debt = $20.92B (2021-12-31).

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality:** High and stable net margin (0.51) and operating margin (0.635) indicate consistent profitability. ROE = 0.409 (40.9%), stable over the window. Cash-flow data unavailable to assess conversion.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Equity base is growing ($38.56B latest). Debt-to-equity ratio not calculable for recent periods due to missing debt data. Historical debt/equity (2021-12-31) = 0.58 ($20.92B / $36.19B). Liabilities decreased QoQ, improving leverage.

**3. Valuation versus Company's Own Supplied History**
- Valuation multiples (P/E, EV/EBIT, FCF yield, P/B) are unavailable. Only margin and ROE history provided: net margin ranged 0.5–0.5, ROE ranged 0.4–0.4 over the 365-day window. No point-in-time valuation comparison possible.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context versus Fundamental Path**
- **Price:** Latest close = $237.48 (2023-06-30), near 52-week high of $250.58, above 52-week low of $174.60.
- **Returns:** Quarterly return (from 2023-03-30) = +6.8%; annual return (from 2022-06-29) = +19.0%.
- **Fundamental Path:** Revenue and earnings grew steadily over the same period. Price recovery from $179.18 (2022-09-28) to $237.48 aligns with improving fundamentals (e.g., revenue from $7.79B to $7.99B, net income from $3.94B to $4.26B).

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
- **Upside:** Consistent revenue growth (+11.1% YoY), high margins (net margin 0.51, operating margin 0.635), and strong ROE (0.409) support earnings durability. Equity growth ($38.56B) enhances balance-sheet strength.
- **Downside:** Absence of cash-flow data limits assessment of earnings quality. Stock near 52-week high ($237.48 vs. $250.58) may reflect priced-in growth. Missing valuation multiples prevent assessment of overvaluation risk.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
- Fundamentals are strong: revenue growth, high margins, and rising equity. However, without cash-flow data or valuation multiples, a full risk/reward assessment is incomplete. The stock’s price appreciation (+19.0% annual) tracks fundamental improvement, but the lack of valuation context means upside/downside cannot be quantitatively balanced.
V.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Analysis: Visa Inc. (V) - Decision Date: 2023-07-01

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 2022-11-16 10-K)**
Visa operates the world's largest digital payments network, facilitating commerce and money movement across over 200 countries. It is a technology company (not a lender) that processes transactions for financial institutions, merchants, and governments through its VisaNet platform. Its revenue is derived from service, data processing, and international transaction fees, net of client incentives. Core strategic pillars include:
*   **Consumer Payments:** Accelerating the shift from cash/checks to digital payments via credit, debit, and prepaid products. Growth enablers include contactless "tap to pay" and security via Visa Token Service.
*   **New Flows:** Expanding beyond traditional consumer-to-business payments into high-growth areas like real-time money movement (Visa Direct) and business-to-business (B2B) payments (e.g., Visa B2B Connect).
*   **Value-Added Services (VAS):** Diversifying revenue with issuing solutions (e.g., Visa DPS), acceptance solutions (e.g., Cybersource), risk/identity tools, open banking (via Tink acquisition), and consulting.
*   **Network of Networks:** Evolving to provide a single connection point for all money movement endpoints across multiple networks and form factors (P2P, B2C, B2B, G2C).

**2. Recent Developments (Point-in-Time from Supplied Text)**
*   **Corporate Action - Acquisition (2023-06-29):** Visa announced the $1 billion acquisition of Pismo, a cloud-native issuer processing and core banking platform. The stated rationale is to enhance its capabilities to provide core banking and issuer processing, expanding its presence in global financial services.
*   **Strategic Partnership (2023-06-28):** Visa joined with SAP to expand its B2B payments footprint in the Asia-Pacific region, aiming to bridge working capital gaps in supply chains and enable faster payments.
*   **Market Sentiment (2023-06-29):** The stock rallied on better-than-expected U.S. economic data (GDP, jobless claims) and news of the Pismo acquisition.
*   **Growth Narrative (2023-06-29):** A web context source cited strong momentum in Value-Added Services, which grew 20% year-over-year in constant currency during Q2.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets**
*   **Risk: Regulatory & Legal Pressure (2023-06-14):** Reports indicated the U.S. Department of Justice is weighing a potential antitrust lawsuit against Visa, citing "regulatory uncertainty." This is compounded by existing merchant-led antitrust litigation referenced in the 10-K.
*   **Offset:** Visa's global scale, brand, and deep integration into the financial system provide significant resilience. Its strategy of partnership (e.g., with fintechs, SAP) and technology investment (e.g., open banking via Tink) is designed to reinforce its network value and adapt to competitive threats.
*   **Risk: Competitive Disruption (2022-11-16 10-K):** The 10-K acknowledges dynamic change in the global payments industry with new entrants and competitors vying for consumers, institutions, and merchants through innovations in ecommerce, mobile, and digital currencies.
*   **Offset:** Visa's extensive global network, transaction scale (193 billion processed transactions in FY2022), and continued investment in digital enablers (contactless, tokens) create high barriers to entry. The "network of networks" strategy positions it to capture new flows beyond traditional cards.
*   **Risk: Macroeconomic Sensitivity (Implicit):** While not a direct lender, Visa's transaction volume is tied to global consumer and business spending, which is influenced by economic conditions.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Positive, with noted regulatory uncertainty.**
Visa maintains a dominant, defensible franchise in global digital payments. Recent strategic actions (Pismo acquisition, SAP partnership) directly support its core growth pillars of New Flows and Value-Added Services. The narrative around strong VAS growth and a stock rally on positive economic data reflects continued execution. However, a material and unresolved qualitative risk is the overhang of potential U.S. antitrust action, which creates regulatory uncertainty and could impact long-term strategic flexibility. The balance between its strong competitive moat and this significant external risk informs the overall positive-but-cautious view.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Visa's dominant global payments franchise delivers consistent double-digit revenue growth (+11.1% YoY), industry-leading margins (51% net, 63.5% operating), and strong ROE (40.9%), supported by strategic expansion into New Flows and Value-Added Services. However, the stock trades near its 52-week high with missing valuation multiples, and a potential U.S. DOJ antitrust lawsuit introduces meaningful regulatory overhang that caps conviction.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Visa's dominant global payments franchise delivers consistent double-digit revenue growth (+11.1% YoY), industry-leading margins (51% net, 63.5% operating), and strong ROE (40.9%), supported by strategic expansion into New Flows and Value-Added Services. However, the stock trades near its 52-week high with missing valuation multiples, and a potential U.S. DOJ antitrust lawsuit introduces meaningful regulatory overhang that caps conviction.
key_factors: Revenue grew +11.1% YoY to TTM $30.98B with net income of $15.79B (+16.7% YoY), reflecting durable secular tailwinds from cash-to-digital payment conversion.
Net margin of 0.51 and operating margin of 0.635 are exceptionally high and stable, with ROE of 0.409, underscoring Visa's asset-light, high-return business model.
The $1B Pismo acquisition (2023-06-29) and SAP B2B partnership (2023-06-28) directly advance the New Flows and Value-Added Services pillars, with VAS growing 20% YoY in constant currency.
U.S. DOJ is reportedly weighing an antitrust lawsuit (2023-06-14), creating material regulatory uncertainty that could constrain strategic flexibility and warrants a valuation discount.

Item 1. Business
Overview
Walgreens Boots Alliance, Inc., a Delaware corporation ("Walgreens Boots Alliance" or the "Company"), is an integrated healthcare, pharmacy and retail leader serving millions of customers and patients every day, with a 170-year heritage of caring for communities. The Company is proud of its contributions to healthy communities, a healthy planet, an inclusive workplace and a sustainable marketplace. Walgreens Boots Alliance is a participant of the United Nations Global Compact and adheres to its principles-based approach to responsible business. The Company has been recognized for its commitment to operating sustainably; it is an index component of the Dow Jones Sustainability Indices ("DJSI") and was named to the 100 Best Corporate Citizens of 2022.
A trusted, global inno
…[truncated 21209 chars]…
nts of the segment's sales are Pharmacy (the sale of prescription drugs and provision of pharmacy-related services) and Retail (the sale of healthcare and retail products including non-prescription drugs, health and wellness, beauty and personal care, and consumables and general merchandise). The segment's sales are subject to the influence of seasonality, particularly the cough, cold and flu seasons and winter holiday. This seasonality also can affect the segment's proportion of sales between Retail and Pharmacy during certain periods. The components of the segment's fiscal year sales were as follows:
|  | Fiscal 2022 | Fiscal 2022 | Fiscal 2021 | Fiscal 2021 | Fiscal 2020 | Fiscal 2020 |
| Pharmacy | 74 | 74% | 76 | 76% | 75 | 75% |
| Retail | 26 | 26% | 24 | 24% | 25 | 25% |
| Total | 100 | 100% | 100 | 100% | 100 | 100% |
The Company filled 819.6 million prescriptions (including vaccinations) in the segment in fiscal 2022. Adjusted to 30-day equivalents, prescriptions filled were 1.2 billion in fiscal 2022. The Company fills prescriptions under Medicare, Medicaid and other publicly financed or sponsored health benefit and prescription drug plans and programs, including the federal 340B drug pricing program. Sales where reimbursement is received from managed care organizations, governmental agencies, PBM companies and private insurance were approximately 97% of the segment's fiscal 2022 Pharmacy sales.
The Company fills prescriptions for many state Medicaid public assistance programs. Sales from all such Medicaid plans were approximately 5% of the segment's fiscal 2022 sales. Sales from Medicare Part D plans were approximately 22% of the segment's fiscal 2022 sales.
The Company's myWalgreens Credit Card program features the myWalgreens Mastercard and the myWalgreens Credit Card. These cards are the first ever of their kind to reward more personalized wellbeing choices and offer industry-leading rewards at Walgreens locations, Walgreens.com, Duane Reade stores, via the Walgreens mobile app, and wherever Mastercard is accepted.
AmerisourceBergen supplies and distributes a significant amount of generic and branded pharmaceutical products to the segment's pharmacies. The Company purchases its non-pharmaceutical merchandise from numerous manufacturers and wholesalers.
The segment's sales, gross profit margin and gross profit are impacted by, among other things, both the percentage of prescriptions filled that are generic and the rate at which new generic drugs are introduced to the market. Because any number of factors outside of the Company's control can affect timing for a generic conversion, the Company faces substantial uncertainty in predicting when such conversions will occur and what effect they will have on particular future periods.
WBA Fiscal 2022 Form 10-K 5
Table of Contents
The current environment of the Company's pharmacy business also includes ongoing reimbursement pressure, a shift in pharmacy mix towards 90-day at retail (one prescription that is the equivalent of three 30-day prescriptions), an increased volume of Medicare Part D prescriptions and increased consumer use of prescription discount cards. Further consolidation among generic manufacturers coupled with changes in the number of major brand name drugs anticipated to undergo a conversion from branded to generic status may also result in gross margin pressures within the industry.
The Company continuously faces reimbursement pressure from PBM companies, government, health maintenance organizations, managed care organizations and other commercial third-party payers. Agreements with these payers are regularly subject to expiration, termination or renegotiation. In addition, plan changes with rate adjustments often occur in January and the Company's reimbursement arrangements may provide for rate adjustments at prescribed intervals during their term. The Company experienced lower reimbursement rates in fiscal 2022 as compared to the same period in the prior year. The Company expects these pressures to continue.
The Company has also worked to develop and expand its relationships with commercial third-party payers to enable new and/or improved market access via participation in pharmacy provider networks they offer. The prescription volume impact of new agreements and relationships typically is incremental over time.
The Company's 90-day at retail prescription drug offering is typically at a lower margin than comparable 30-day prescriptions, but provides the Company with the opportunity to increase business with patients with chronic prescription needs while offering increased convenience, helping facilitate improved prescription adherence and resulting in a lower cost to fill the 90-day prescription. Similarly, the specialty prescription business, which generates higher sales dollars per prescription, may result in gross margin pressures within the industry, as compared to generic prescription drugs. The segment's performance is also impacted by the current environment, including the uncertainty as a result of COVID-19. For more information, see Risk factors in Item 1A.
International
The International segment consists of pharmacy-led health and beauty retail businesses outside the U.S. and the pharmaceutical wholesaling and distribution business in Germany.
Pharmacy-led health and beauty retail businesses include Boots branded stores in the United Kingdom ("UK"), the Republic of Ireland and Thailand, the Benavides brand in Mexico and the Ahumada brand in Chile. Sales for these businesses are principally derived from the sale of prescription drugs and health and wellness, beauty, personal care and other consumer products. The Company operated 3,989 retail stores in the segment as of August 31, 2022 (see properties in Part I, Item 2 for information regarding geographic coverage) and has grown its omni-channel platform, including its online presence, in recent years. In the UK, the Company is a market leader and its retail stores are conveniently located with pharmacists well placed to provide a significant role in the provision of healthcare services, working closely with other primary healthcare providers in the communities the Company serves.
The Boots omni-channel offering is differentiated from that of competitors due to the product brands the Company owns, such as No7, Liz Earle, Soap & Glory, Botanics, Sleek MakeUp, Boots Pharmaceuticals and 'only at Boots' exclusive products, together with its long established reputation for trust and customer care. The Company's brands portfolio is enhanced by its in-house product research and development capabilities. The Company has introduced new beauty brands and beauty halls in key locations. Certain of the product brands of the Company are also sold by third-party retailers.
The Company's retail store networks are typically complemented by online platforms. In the UK, through the boots.com website and integrated mobile application, the 'order and collect' service normally allows customers to order from a range of over 37,000 products by 5:00 p.m. and collect the following day from approximately 98% of the UK's retail stores.
The Boots Advantage Card loyalty program, where customers earn points on purchases for redemption at a later date, continues to be a key element of the Boots offering. As of August 31, 2022, the number of active Boots Advantage Card members (members who have used their card in the last six months) totaled approximately 13 million.
In addition, Boots in the UK is one of the leaders in the optical market with 543 practices, of which 160 operated on a franchise basis as of August 31, 2022. Approximately 30% of these optical practices are located in Boots stores with the balance being standalone optical practices.

WBA.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: WBA as of 2023-07-01**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
*   **Revenue:** Shows slight growth. Latest Q3 (2023-05-31) revenue was $35.41B, up +8.6% YoY from $32.60B. TTM revenue is $136.11B.
*   **Earnings & Margins:** Deteriorating. Latest Q3 net income was -$48.00M (EPS=$0.14). This is down QoQ from net income of $544.00M (EPS=$0.81) in Q2. The TTM net income is -$3.73B. TTM operating income is -$6.85B. Latest quarterly gross margin is 18.6% ($6.59B/$35.41B); operating margin is negative at -1.3%.
*   **Cash-Flow:** Data not available for any period within the 8-quarter lookback.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Equity is shrinking. Latest total equity is $29.36B versus total assets of $98.61B, implying a leverage ratio (Assets/Equity) of ~3.36. Equity has declined from $30.38B in Q3 2022 to the current level. Total liabilities stand at $69.09B.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** Poor. Earnings are highly volatile and recently negative. The TTM net loss of -$3.73B is driven by a large operating loss of -$6.85B. The trajectory shows sharp swings: EPS ranged from -$4.31 (Q1 2023) to $4.13 (Q1 2022). The latest quarter's minimal positive EPS ($0.14) follows a quarter with a large loss.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Strained. The company carries significant leverage ($69.09B liabilities vs. $29.36B equity). Total debt was $11.71B as of 2022-08-31 (the latest point with debt data). The negative operating income and declining equity trend indicate a weakening buffer.

**3. Valuation Versus Supplied History**
*   **Current Valuation:** Attractive multiples on traditional metrics, but negative on earnings. Latest P/B ratio is 0.838 and P/S ratio is 0.181. The P/E ratio is not meaningful due to negative trailing earnings. Net margin is -0.028 and ROE is -0.13.
*   **Historical Context:** The current P/B of 0.838 is at the low end of its 1-year range (0.8 to 1.3). One year prior (2022-07-01), P/B was 1.098 and ROE was positive 0.17. The multiple has compressed alongside the deterioration in fundamental returns.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**
*   **Price Level:** $28.49 as of 2023-06-30. The 52-week range is $28.14 to $42.29, placing the current price near the yearly low.
*   **Return Context:** Price returns are deeply negative, aligning with the fundamental decline. The quarterly return (approx. 63 days) is -17.7%. The annual return (approx. 252 days) is -30.3%. The price has fallen from $40.87 (2022-06-29) and $37.58 (2022-12-28) to current levels.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
*   **Upside:** A potential mean-reversion in valuation multiples if earnings stabilize. The stock trades at a P/B of 0.838, well below its 1-year average range.
*   **Downside:** Continued erosion of equity and earnings, further leveraging the balance sheet. The TTM operating loss of -$6.85B and negative operating margin of -5.3% highlight the core profitability challenge. The absence of cash flow data is a significant gap in assessing sustainability.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The fundamental trajectory is negative, characterized by revenue stagnation (QoQ +1.6%), volatile and deeply negative earnings (TTM net loss -$3.73B), and a leveraged, shrinking equity base (leverage ~3.36x). Valuation multiples are historically low (P/B 0.838) but reflect this deterioration, as ROE is -13%. Price performance (-30.3% annual) tracks the fundamental decline. The risk is continued fundamental weakness; the reward hinges on a turnaround not evidenced in the current numbers. The quantitative profile shows significant fundamental stress with low valuation providing no clear margin of safety given the negative earnings and missing cash flow data.
WBA.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Report: Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA)
**Decision Date:** 2023-07-01

#### 1. Business/Franchise Read
Based on the 2022 10-K (filing_date=2022-10-13), Walgreens Boots Alliance is a global integrated healthcare, pharmacy, and retail leader with approximately 13,000 locations across the U.S., Europe, and Latin America. Its operations are conducted through three segments: U.S. Retail Pharmacy (sales of $109.1B in fiscal 2022), International ($21.8B), and U.S. Healthcare ($1.8B). The company operates as a neighborhood health destination, dispensing prescriptions and providing retail and health services. It has made significant strategic investments to build a U.S. Healthcare platform through majority-owned businesses including primary care (VillageMD), specialty pharmacy (Shields Health Solutions), and post-acute/home care (CareCentrix). The franchise is supported by a large loyalty program (myWalgreens with ~102 million members), an omni-channel digital platform, and a significant equity investment in pharmaceutical wholesaler AmerisourceBergen.

#### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)
*   **Earnings & Guidance:
    *   2023-06-28: Reported weaker-than-expected Q3 profits and **lowered its FY23 adjusted EPS guidance to $4.00-$4.05 from the prior $4.45-$4.65**, citing consumer and category conditions, lower COVID-19 contribution, and a more cautious macroeconomic view.
    *   2023-06-28: The disappointing quarter and outlook led analysts to hold a cautious stance, as summarized in the headline "Hold Your Horses: Analysts Sit On Fence For Walgreens Boots Alliance."
*   **Analyst Reaction:
    *   2023-06-28: Barclays **maintained an Equal-Weight rating but lowered its price target from $38.00 to $32.00** following the results.
*   **Corporate Strategy & Action:
    *   2023-06-27 (per web context summary): The company announced it is **raising its Transformational Cost Management Program target from $3.5 billion to $4.1 billion in cumulative savings by fiscal 2024**.
*   **Legal & Settlements:
    *   2023-05-18: Walgreens **reached a $229 million opioid settlement** with the City and County of San Francisco, following a finding that it contributed to a public nuisance.
    *   2023-05-23: Walgreens **asked a federal court to vacate a $642 million arbitration award** to Humana in a drug-pricing dispute, calling it "staggering."
*   **Community Engagement:
    *   2023-06-29: Announced it will sponsor a diabetes care event, aligning with its healthcare strategy for the 122 million individuals with diabetes/prediabetes.

#### 3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets
**Risks:**
*   **Continued Reimbursement Pressure:** The 10-K states the company "continuously faces reimbursement pressure" from PBMs, governments, and other payers, and expects it to continue. This is a structural headwind to pharmacy margins.
*   **Regulatory Environment:** The 2022 Inflation Reduction Act introduces new policies to control federal drug spending, which could complicate pricing and impact profitability for the company (noted in 10-K, filing 2022-10-13).
*   **Opioid Litigation & Settlements:** Recent settlements (e.g., $229M with San Francisco on 2023-05-18) and a disputed $642M arbitration (2023-05-23) represent significant ongoing financial and legal risks.
*   **Execution Risk:** The company is integrating majority investments in VillageMD, Shields, and CareCentrix to build its U.S. Healthcare segment. These are capital-intensive, and turning them into profitable growth drivers is an ongoing challenge, reflected in the lowered earnings outlook.
*   **Competitive Pressures:** The industry is described as "highly competitive and dynamic." News on 2023-06-29 about competitor Rite Aid's debt restructuring talks highlights the difficult operating environment for retail pharmacies.
*   **Consumer Sensitivity:** Management's guidance cut explicitly cited weaker consumer and category conditions (2023-06-28), indicating sensitivity to the macroeconomic environment.

**Offsets:**
*   **Structural Cost Control:** The company is proactively raising its cost management target to $4.1B in savings by FY2024 (2023-06-27), aiming to offset some margin pressures.
*   **Strategic Pivot to Healthcare:** Investments in primary care, specialty, and home health create a long-term pathway beyond traditional pharmacy, leveraging its vast retail footprint and patient relationships (per 10-K strategy).
*   **Scale and Reach:** With a network where ~78% of the U.S. population lives within five miles of a store and over 100 million loyalty members, the franchise has a significant distribution and customer data advantage.
*   **Generics & Volume Trends:** The business benefits from long-term tailwinds like aging demographics and the continued shift to higher-volume, lower-cost generic drugs (per 10-K).

#### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Negative.** The point-in-time evidence through mid-2023 presents a concerning picture of near-term operational stress. The **significant lowering of FY2023 earnings guidance** (2023-06-28) is the most salient development, reflecting tangible pressure from consumers and lower COVID-related activity. This was met with analyst skepticism and price target cuts. Simultaneously, the company faces substantial **overhang from opioid-related legal settlements and disputes**. While the company is taking decisive action through cost management and executing on its long-term healthcare integration strategy, these positives are overshadowed in the near term by material risks in reimbursement, regulation, and competition. The qualitative evidence points to a business in a challenging transition period with clear execution hurdles.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: WBA is a structurally challenged retail pharmacy in a painful transition, with deeply negative TTM earnings, a guidance cut, and ongoing legal overhangs. While the stock trades at a historically low P/B of 0.838, the discount is warranted by deteriorating fundamentals and missing cash flow visibility, offering no clear margin of safety.

FY23 adjusted EPS guidance was slashed to $4.00-$4.05 from $4.45-$4.65 on 2023-06-28, citing weaker consumer conditions and lower COVID contribution, with Barclays cutting its price target to $32.

Opioid litigation continues to extract material financial toll: a $229M San Francisco settlement (2023-05-18) and a disputed $642M Humana arbitration award (2023-05-23) represent ongoing balance-sheet risk.

Balance sheet is strained with $69.09B in liabilities against $29.36B equity (leverage ~3.36x), and equity has declined from $30.38B a year ago, while cash flow data is unavailable to assess debt service sustainability.
